news_summary: Breakthroughs in quantum security, advanced semiconductor technology, cellular rejuvenation, and antimatter transportation showcase remarkable progress in physics and material science.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans les domaines de la sécurité quantique, des technologies de pointe en matière de semi-conducteurs, du rajeunissement cellulaire et du transport de l'antimatière témoignent des progrès remarquables réalisés en physique et en science des matériaux.
news_summary_es: Los avances en seguridad cuántica, tecnología avanzada de semiconductores, rejuvenecimiento celular y transporte de antimateria ponen de manifiesto los notables progresos realizados en física y ciencia de los materiales.

articles.title: Physicists just turned glass into a powerful quantum security device
articles.summary: Scientists have turned simple glass into a powerful quantum communication device that could safeguard data against future quantum attacks. The chip combines stability, speed, and versatility—handling both ultra-secure encryption and record-breaking random number generation in one compact system.
articles.content:
As quantum computers grow more powerful, many current encryption methods could eventually become vulnerable. One promising solution is quantum cryptography, which relies on the laws of physics rather than mathematical complexity to keep data secure. However, making quantum communication practical requires small, dependable devices that can accurately read delicate quantum signals carried by light.

Researchers from the University of Padua, Politecnico di Milano, and the CNR Institute for Photonics and Nanotechnologies have demonstrated a new approach using an unexpected material: borosilicate glass. Reported in Advanced Photonics, their study describes a high-performance quantum coherent receiver built directly inside glass using femtosecond laser writing. This method delivers low optical loss, stable performance, and compatibility with existing fiber-optic systems, all of which are important for moving quantum technologies beyond lab experiments.

Why Glass Outperforms Silicon in Quantum Devices

Continuous-variable (CV) quantum information processing, used in quantum key distribution (QKD) and quantum random number generation (QRNG), depends on measuring the amplitude and phase of light waves. To do this, a coherent receiver combines a weak quantum signal with a stronger reference beam and analyzes how they interfere.

Most current integrated receivers are made from silicon. While silicon is widely used and supports high integration, it is sensitive to polarization and tends to have higher optical losses, which can limit performance and reliability in quantum systems.

Glass offers several advantages. It is naturally insensitive to polarization, highly stable, and allows waveguides to be written in three dimensions with minimal signal loss. Using femtosecond laser micromachining, researchers can create light-guiding paths directly inside the material, forming compact photonic circuits without the complexity of semiconductor manufacturing.

Inside the Laser-Written Quantum Receiver

The team created a fully tunable heterodyne receiver, a key component for CV-QKD and CV-QRNG, by writing the optical circuit directly inside borosilicate glass. The chip includes:

Fixed and tunable beam splitters

Thermo-optic phase shifters for precise electrical control

Three-dimensional waveguide crossings

Polarization-independent directional couplers

These features allow the quantum signal and reference beam to interact in a controlled way, enabling simultaneous measurement of two conjugate quadratures. The device also shows:

Extremely low insertion loss (≈1 dB)

Polarization-independent operation

Common-mode rejection ratio above 73 dB, indicating strong suppression of classical noise

Stable signal-to-noise performance over at least 8 hours

Overall, these results match or exceed the performance of many silicon-based photonic receivers.

One Chip, Two Quantum Technologies

Because the device combines low loss, tunability, and stability, it can handle multiple quantum communication tasks without requiring different hardware. When used as a heterodyne detector, it enabled a source-device-independent QRNG system, meaning it remains secure even if the incoming optical signal cannot be trusted. The chip achieved a secure random bit generation rate of 42.7 Gbit/s, setting a record for this type of system.

The same chip was also used for a QPSK-based CV-QKD protocol, where information is encoded in a four-point constellation of quantum states. In a simulated 9.3-km fiber link, the system reached a 3.2 Mbit/s secret key rate. These results show that a glass-based photonic front end can support advanced CV-QKD without the drawbacks seen in silicon platforms.

Glass Photonics Moves Toward Real-World Use

In addition to strong performance, the study highlights several practical benefits of using glass in integrated quantum photonics:

Environmental stability: Glass is inert and resistant to temperature and mechanical changes.

Low-loss fiber coupling: Waveguides closely match standard telecom fiber sizes.

3D design flexibility: Circuits can include crossings and complex layouts without added signal loss.

Scalability and cost-effectiveness: Femtosecond laser writing allows rapid prototyping without expensive semiconductor fabrication.

These qualities support long-term reliability and durability, which are important for real-world deployment and even potential use in space-based quantum communication systems. The researchers note that glass-based photonics could help close the gap between experimental setups and practical quantum networks.

Toward Scalable Quantum Communication Networks

By leveraging these advantages, the team demonstrated two major applications on a single chip: a source-device-independent QRNG with a record-high secure generation rate of 42.7 Gbit/s, and a QPSK-based CV-QKD system achieving a 3.2 Mbit/s secure key rate over a simulated 9.3-kilometer fiber link.

Beyond these results, the work points to glass-based integrated photonics as a durable and versatile platform for future quantum technologies. Glass is stable, cost-effective, and resistant to harsh environments, making it well suited for scalable deployment. This approach could help transition quantum communication from controlled lab settings to real-world infrastructure, marking an important step toward building global quantum networks.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in quantum communication technology by developing a stable, low-loss, and versatile quantum receiver chip made from glass. This innovation addresses key challenges in quantum cryptography, enabling practical, scalable, and secure quantum communication systems with broad societal impact in data security. The detailed description of the device's capabilities, performance metrics, and potential real-world applications demonstrates substantial substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create glass chip to boost quantum communication security
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As quantum computers improve, they could break many current ways of protecting data, called encryption. To keep information safe in the future, scientists are developing quantum cryptography, which uses the rules of physics instead of math problems to secure data. But making this technology practical needs small, reliable devices that can handle delicate quantum signals carried by light.

**What happened** – Researchers in Italy made a new quantum device using borosilicate glass, a common type of glass. They used a special laser technique to write tiny circuits inside the glass, creating a quantum receiver chip. This chip can measure light signals very precisely, and it works better than many silicon-based devices because it loses less light and is more stable. The device can do two important jobs: create super-secure random numbers and send secret keys for encryption.

**Impact** – This glass chip can generate secure random numbers at a record speed of 42.7 billion bits per second and can send secret keys over nearly 10 kilometers of fiber optic cable. It works well for hours without losing quality and is less affected by changes in temperature or movement. These qualities make it easier to use quantum communication outside the lab, for example, in real-world networks or even in space.

**What's next step** – The researchers aim to use this glass technology to build larger, practical quantum communication networks. Because the chip is stable, cost-effective, and easy to produce, it could help companies and governments protect data with quantum security in everyday situations. This work brings quantum communication closer to being a regular part of internet and phone networks.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new glass chip offers a stable, efficient way to secure data with quantum technology, making future-proof communication more practical and reliable.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent une puce en verre pour renforcer la sécurité quantique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – À mesure que les ordinateurs quantiques progressent, ils pourraient casser de nombreuses méthodes actuelles de protection des données, appelées chiffrement. Pour garder les informations sécurisées à l'avenir, les scientifiques développent la cryptographie quantique, qui utilise les lois de la physique au lieu des problèmes mathématiques. Mais pour que cette technologie soit pratique, il faut des appareils petits et fiables capables de gérer des signaux quantiques délicats portés par la lumière.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs italiens ont fabriqué un nouveau dispositif quantique en utilisant du verre borosilicaté, un type de verre courant. Ils ont utilisé une technique laser spéciale pour écrire de minuscules circuits à l'intérieur du verre, créant une puce réceptrice quantique. Cette puce peut mesurer très précisément les signaux lumineux, et elle fonctionne mieux que beaucoup d'appareils en silicium car elle perd moins de lumière et est plus stable. Le dispositif peut réaliser deux tâches importantes : créer des nombres aléatoires ultra-sécurisés et envoyer des clés secrètes pour le chiffrement.

**Impact** – Cette puce en verre peut générer des nombres aléatoires sécurisés à une vitesse record de 42,7 milliards de bits par seconde et peut envoyer des clés secrètes sur près de 10 kilomètres de fibre optique. Elle fonctionne bien pendant des heures sans perdre en qualité et est moins sensible aux changements de température ou de mouvement. Ces qualités facilitent l'utilisation de la communication quantique en dehors du laboratoire, par exemple dans des réseaux réels ou même dans l'espace.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent utiliser cette technologie en verre pour construire des réseaux de communication quantique plus larges et pratiques. Comme la puce est stable, économique et facile à produire, elle pourrait aider les entreprises et les gouvernements à protéger leurs données avec la sécurité quantique dans la vie quotidienne. Ce travail rapproche la communication quantique d'une utilisation courante dans les réseaux internet et téléphoniques.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Une nouvelle puce en verre offre une méthode stable et efficace pour sécuriser les données avec la technologie quantique, rendant la communication à l'épreuve du futur plus pratique et fiable.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean chip de vidrio para mejorar la seguridad cuántica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que las computadoras cuánticas avanzan, podrían romper muchas formas actuales de proteger datos, llamadas cifrado. Para mantener la información segura en el futuro, los científicos desarrollan la criptografía cuántica, que usa las leyes de la física en lugar de problemas matemáticos. Pero para que esta tecnología sea práctica, se necesitan dispositivos pequeños y confiables que puedan manejar señales cuánticas delicadas que viajan por la luz.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en Italia crearon un nuevo dispositivo cuántico usando vidrio de borosilicato, un tipo común de vidrio. Usaron una técnica láser especial para escribir circuitos diminutos dentro del vidrio, creando un chip receptor cuántico. Este chip puede medir señales de luz con mucha precisión y funciona mejor que muchos dispositivos de silicio porque pierde menos luz y es más estable. El dispositivo puede hacer dos trabajos importantes: crear números aleatorios súper seguros y enviar claves secretas para cifrado.

**Impacto** – Este chip de vidrio puede generar números aleatorios seguros a una velocidad récord de 42.7 mil millones de bits por segundo y puede enviar claves secretas a casi 10 kilómetros de fibra óptica. Funciona bien durante horas sin perder calidad y es menos afectado por cambios de temperatura o movimiento. Estas cualidades facilitan el uso de la comunicación cuántica fuera del laboratorio, por ejemplo, en redes reales o incluso en el espacio.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren usar esta tecnología de vidrio para construir redes de comunicación cuántica más grandes y prácticas. Como el chip es estable, económico y fácil de producir, podría ayudar a empresas y gobiernos a proteger datos con seguridad cuántica en situaciones cotidianas. Este trabajo acerca la comunicación cuántica a ser parte regular de internet y redes telefónicas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo chip de vidrio ofrece una forma estable y eficiente de proteger datos con tecnología cuántica, haciendo la comunicación a prueba de futuro más práctica y confiable.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a translucent borosilicate glass chip glowing softly from within, with delicate, three-dimensional light pathways weaving through it like glowing threads, intersecting and splitting gently to symbolize quantum signals and reference beams; surrounding the chip, subtle fibers extend outward connecting it seamlessly to a calm, abstract network, all rendered in natural soft blues, warm ambers, and gentle whites to evoke stability, precision, and the quiet power of quantum communication.

articles.title: New light trap design supercharges atom-thin semiconductors
articles.summary: Scientists have found a clever way to supercharge ultra-thin semiconductors by reshaping the space beneath them rather than altering the material itself. By placing a single-atom-thick layer of tungsten disulfide over tiny air cavities carved into a crystal, they created miniature “light traps” that dramatically boost brightness and optical effects—up to 20 times stronger emission and 25 times stronger nonlinear signals. These hollow structures, called Mie voids, concentrate light exactly where the material sits, overcoming a major limitation of atomically thin devices.
articles.content:
Atomically thin semiconductors like tungsten disulfide (WS 2 ) are emerging as key materials for next-generation photonic technologies. Even though they are only a single layer of atoms, they can host tightly bound excitons, which are electron and hole pairs that interact strongly with light. These materials can also produce new colors of light through nonlinear optical effects such as second-harmonic generation. Because of these properties, they are promising for applications in quantum optics, sensing, and compact on-chip light sources. However, their extreme thinness also creates a challenge. With so little material available, light has limited interaction, which often results in weak emission and inefficient frequency conversion unless the surrounding photonic environment is carefully designed.

A study published in Advanced Photonics presents a new strategy to overcome this limitation by modifying not the material itself, but the space beneath it. In this approach, a single layer of WS 2 is placed on nanoscale air cavities, called Mie voids, which are carved into a high-index crystal of bismuth telluride (Bi 2 Te 3 ). These tiny voids significantly boost light emission and nonlinear optical signals. They also make it possible to directly observe localized optical modes, offering new insight into how light behaves at very small scales.

Turning Empty Space Into a Light Resonator

Traditional dielectric nanoresonators trap light inside solid materials like silicon. While effective in many cases, this design keeps the strongest optical fields away from the surface where atomically thin materials sit. It also becomes less efficient when the material absorbs light, which weakens the resonance and reduces field intensity.

Mie voids work differently. Instead of trapping light inside solid matter, they confine it within subwavelength air cavities etched into a material with a very high refractive index. Strong reflections at the air-dielectric boundary keep the light circulating inside the cavity. As a result, the optical field is concentrated in the air region and near the top surface, exactly where the WS 2 layer is located.

This "inverted" confinement approach offers several benefits. The enhanced field is directly accessible to surface materials, the resonant wavelength can be tuned by adjusting the cavity shape, and the design remains effective even in materials that strongly absorb light. Bi2Te₃, which is not ideal for conventional resonators, performs well in this void-based configuration.

Designing and Building the Structure

Using detailed electromagnetic simulations, the researchers designed cavities that support a dipolar resonance aligned with the main emission feature of WS 2 , known as the A-exciton. By carefully adjusting the radius and depth of each cavity, they could control both the resonance wavelength and the vertical position of the optical mode.

The cavities were created using focused ion beam milling in thick, mechanically exfoliated Bi 2 Te 3 flakes. They were spaced far enough apart to function as individual resonators rather than interacting with one another. A continuous WS 2 monolayer was then transferred across the patterned surface, covering resonant cavities, non-resonant cavities, and flat regions. This design ensured that any differences in optical behavior were due to the cavity geometry and not variations in the material itself.

Optical reflection measurements confirmed that the cavities behaved as expected. Larger cavities caused a smooth shift of the resonance toward longer wavelengths, while changes in depth altered both the spectral position and the vertical location of the optical mode. Importantly, the resonances remained stable even when the geometry was not perfectly optimized, showing that the design is tolerant of fabrication imperfections.

Boosting Light Emission From WS 2

To understand how the cavities affect light emission, the team measured photoluminescence from WS 2 under laser excitation while varying the cavity depth. When the cavity resonance matched the WS 2 emission band, the light output increased by about 20 times compared to the least resonant cavity.

Further analysis showed that this boost was not due to stronger absorption of the incoming light. Simulations indicated no significant enhancement at the excitation wavelength, and experiments using different pump wavelengths consistently produced the strongest emission at the same cavity depth. This confirms that the improvement comes from emission-related effects. The resonant cavity increases the local optical density of states and helps emitted light escape more efficiently.

Because the WS 2 layer was continuous across the sample, researchers could directly compare emission from different regions under identical conditions. This demonstrated that the enhanced emission was driven by the engineered cavity modes rather than differences in the material itself.

Nonlinear Optics and Visualizing Light Modes

The team also explored nonlinear optical effects by adjusting the cavity geometry so that the resonance shifted into the near-infrared range. Under these conditions, the second-harmonic signal from WS 2 increased by about 25 times compared to non-resonant cavities. The signal peaked when the excitation wavelength aligned with the cavity resonance.

In addition to boosting performance, the system allows direct visualization of optical modes. Far-field imaging of the second-harmonic signal revealed bright, localized hotspots above individual cavities. As the excitation wavelength or cavity depth changed, these hotspots moved in a predictable pattern across the array. This provided a clear, real-space view of how optical fields evolve inside individual resonators, without requiring specialized near-field techniques.

A New Platform for Atom-Thin Photonics

By combining adjustable optical enhancement with precise spatial control in a van der Waals-compatible system, Mie-void heterostructures offer a powerful new platform for working with atomically thin materials. Unlike traditional approaches, this method does not rely on large metasurfaces and remains effective even in materials that strongly absorb light.

This technology could enable advances in nonlinear light generation, surface-enhanced sensing, and programmable photonic devices based on two-dimensional semiconductors. More broadly, it shows that shaping empty space can be just as important as selecting the right material when designing nanoscale light-matter interactions.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in enhancing the performance of atom-thin semiconductors by innovatively reshaping the space beneath them. This advancement has broad implications for next-generation photonic technologies, including quantum optics, sensing, and compact light sources, which can benefit a wide range of applications and industries. The detailed explanation of the method and its impact demonstrates substantial substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: New light traps boost brightness in atom-thin semiconductors
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are working with atom-thin materials like tungsten disulfide (WS2), which are only one atom thick. These materials are important because they interact with light in special ways, useful for future technologies like tiny light sources and sensors. But because they are so thin, they don’t produce much light on their own.

**What happened** – Researchers found a new way to make these thin materials shine much brighter. They carved tiny air-filled cavities, called Mie voids, into a crystal beneath the WS2 layer. These cavities trap and focus light right where the thin material sits. When the WS2 was placed over these cavities, its light emission became up to 20 times stronger. They also saw a 25 times increase in special light effects called nonlinear signals.

**Impact** – This method is unique because it improves light interaction without changing the thin material itself. Instead, it reshapes the space underneath. This solves a big problem for atom-thin semiconductors, which usually struggle to emit strong light. The approach also lets scientists see how light behaves in tiny spaces, helping them understand and control light better at very small scales.

**What's next step** – The discovery opens new possibilities for making better light-based devices using atom-thin materials. It could lead to improved sensors, new ways to generate light colors, and compact optical technologies for computers or communication. Researchers will likely explore different cavity designs and materials to expand these effects and create practical devices.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By carving tiny air cavities beneath atom-thin semiconductors, scientists greatly boosted their light emission and opened new paths for advanced light technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles cavités lumineuses améliorent la brillance des semi-conducteurs atomiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques travaillent avec des matériaux d’une épaisseur d’un seul atome, comme le disulfure de tungstène (WS2). Ces matériaux sont importants car ils interagissent de manière spéciale avec la lumière, utiles pour des technologies futures comme des sources de lumière miniatures et des capteurs. Mais à cause de leur très faible épaisseur, ils produisent peu de lumière.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont trouvé une nouvelle façon de rendre ces matériaux plus lumineux. Ils ont creusé de minuscules cavités remplies d’air, appelées vides de Mie, dans un cristal sous la couche de WS2. Ces cavités piègent et concentrent la lumière juste là où se trouve le matériau fin. Quand le WS2 est placé sur ces cavités, son émission lumineuse devient jusqu’à 20 fois plus forte. Ils ont aussi observé une augmentation par 25 de certains effets lumineux spéciaux appelés signaux non linéaires.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est unique car elle améliore l’interaction avec la lumière sans modifier le matériau fin lui-même. Elle modifie plutôt l’espace en dessous. Cela résout un gros problème des semi-conducteurs atomiques, qui ont souvent du mal à émettre une lumière forte. Cette approche permet aussi aux scientifiques de mieux voir et comprendre comment la lumière se comporte dans des espaces très petits.

**Prochaine étape** – Cette découverte ouvre de nouvelles possibilités pour créer de meilleurs dispositifs lumineux avec des matériaux atomiques. Elle pourrait mener à des capteurs améliorés, de nouvelles façons de générer des couleurs de lumière, et des technologies optiques compactes pour l’informatique ou la communication. Les chercheurs vont probablement explorer différents designs de cavités et matériaux pour étendre ces effets et créer des appareils pratiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En creusant de petites cavités d’air sous des semi-conducteurs atomiques, les scientifiques ont fortement augmenté leur émission lumineuse et ouvert la voie à des technologies optiques avancées.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas trampas de luz aumentan el brillo en semiconductores de una sola capa atómica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos trabajan con materiales tan delgados como un solo átomo, como el disulfuro de tungsteno (WS2). Estos materiales son importantes porque interactúan de manera especial con la luz, lo que es útil para tecnologías futuras como fuentes de luz pequeñas y sensores. Pero debido a su extrema delgadez, producen poca luz por sí solos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron una nueva forma de hacer que estos materiales delgados brillen mucho más. Grabaron pequeñas cavidades llenas de aire, llamadas vacíos de Mie, en un cristal debajo de la capa de WS2. Estas cavidades atrapan y concentran la luz justo donde está el material delgado. Cuando el WS2 se colocó sobre estas cavidades, su emisión de luz fue hasta 20 veces más fuerte. También vieron un aumento de 25 veces en efectos especiales de luz llamados señales no lineales.

**Impacto** – Este método es único porque mejora la interacción con la luz sin cambiar el material delgado. En cambio, modifica el espacio debajo. Esto resuelve un gran problema de los semiconductores de una sola capa, que suelen tener dificultad para emitir luz fuerte. Además, permite a los científicos ver cómo se comporta la luz en espacios muy pequeños, ayudándoles a entender y controlar mejor la luz.

**Próximo paso** – Este descubrimiento abre nuevas posibilidades para crear dispositivos de luz mejores usando materiales de una sola capa atómica. Podría llevar a sensores mejorados, nuevas formas de generar colores de luz y tecnologías ópticas compactas para computadoras o comunicaciones. Los investigadores probablemente explorarán diferentes diseños de cavidades y materiales para ampliar estos efectos y crear dispositivos prácticos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Al grabar pequeñas cavidades de aire bajo semiconductores de una sola capa, los científicos aumentaron mucho su emisión de luz y abrieron nuevas rutas para tecnologías ópticas avanzadas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate, glowing single-layer crystal sheet floating above an array of intricately carved, hollowed-out gemstone-like cavities emitting soft, swirling light waves that concentrate upward into the thin layer, symbolizing enhanced light emission and resonance within atom-thin semiconductor materials.

articles.title: ‘Zombie cells’ return from the dead — after a genome transplant
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00938-6Technique that inserts the genome of one bacterial species into the ‘dead’ cells of another could open doors for synthetic biologists.
articles.content:
These Mycoplasma capricolum bacterial cells have absorbed a genome engineered from a closely related bacterium, Mycoplasma mycoides.Credit: Thomas Deerinck, NCMIR/Science Photo Library

Researchers have resurrected ‘dead’ bacterial cells by replacing their defunct DNA with the working genome of another species.

AI can write genomes — how long until it creates synthetic life?

The feat — reported on the preprint server bioRxiv this month1 — could boost efforts to re-engineer microbial life by moving entire genomes into bacteria to imbue them with useful properties, such as making drugs or biofuels.

Such genome transfers, including the one that gave rise to these ‘zombie cells’, have so far been accomplished only between species within a single bacterial class. But if researchers can routinely make zombies from other bacteria, the approach could be used to test engineered genomes from more commonly studied species, such as the laboratory staple Escherichia coli.

“For me, this paper represents a significant step forward for genome engineering in synthetic biology,” says Olivier Borkowski, a synthetic biologist at the French National Research Institute for Agriculture, Food and Environment (INRAE) and Paris-Saclay University.

Dawn of the dead

More than 15 years ago, researchers chemically synthesized the 1.1-million base-pair genome of the bacterium Mycoplasma mycoides and transplanted it into living cells of the closely related species Mycoplasma capricolum2, creating what they called the first synthetic cell.

‘Minimal’ cell raises stakes in race to harness synthetic life

The team, which included some researchers from the latest study, added a gene to the synthetic M. mycoides genome that imparted resistance to the antibiotic tetracycline. This meant that if the researchers transplanted the genome into M. capricolum and then grew those recipient cells in the presence of tetracycline, the cells would survive only if they had successfully absorbed the synthetic genome — a sign that the test had worked.

A 2016 study successfully transplanted genomes between species in the same class of bacteria as Mycoplasma, called Mollicutes3. But efforts to transplant genomes more widely have failed, says John Glass, a synthetic biologist at the J. Craig Venter Institute (JCVI) in La Jolla, California, and an author of both the new preprint and the synthetic-cell study. Apparent successes with other bacteria turned out to be false positives because the genomes of the recipient cells were able to incorporate antibiotic-resistance genes through a process called homologous recombination. That meant that the recipient cells would survive even if they didn’t absorb the entire donor genome. (M. capricolum didn’t give false positives in the experiments 15 years ago because it doesn’t have this recombination ability.)

In their latest experiment, JCVI researchers killed M. capricolum cells and then resurrected them by inserting a synthetic genome from another bacterial species. The blue colony here is one that's expressing that foreign genome.Credit: Nacyra Assad-Garcia

In search of a way to transplant genomes without having to worry generally about false positives, Glass and his colleagues reported in their preprint this month inactivating the genomes of recipient cells, leaving the cells unable to replicate, or functionally dead. This also prevents the recipient genomes from incorporating foreign DNA such as resistance genes through recombination. The team created these ‘dead’ M. capricolum cells by treating them with a DNA-damaging chemotherapy drug called mitomycin C.

When engineered M. mycoides genomes were transferred into M. capricolum cells treated with mitomycin C, a small fraction of the recipient cells survived. “The cell is destined to die, but we give it life,” says co-author Zumra Peksaglam Seidel, a synthetic biologist at the JCVI. She and her colleagues call these survivors ‘zombie cells’ in their preprint, which has not been peer-reviewed.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in synthetic biology where researchers successfully resurrected dead bacterial cells by transplanting genomes from another species. This advancement could enable broad applications in genome engineering, such as producing drugs or biofuels, with meaningful benefits for science and society. The story focuses on a single, well-explained scientific achievement with clear potential for large-scale impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists revive dead bacteria by swapping their DNA
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been working on changing bacteria by replacing their DNA with new genetic material from other bacteria. This helps create bacteria that can do useful jobs, like making medicine or fuel. But it has been hard to do this with many types of bacteria.

**What happened** – A team of researchers at the J. Craig Venter Institute found a way to bring dead bacterial cells back to life by putting in a complete genome from a different species. They used a chemical to stop the original bacteria from working, then inserted new DNA from a related bacterium. Some of these “dead” cells started working again, growing and acting like normal bacteria.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows a new method to change bacteria more safely and accurately. It stops false results that happened before when bacteria could mix DNA in unexpected ways. Now scientists can try this with different bacteria, including common lab bacteria like E. coli, which means more chances to create helpful bacteria for medicine or energy.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will try this genome transplant method with other kinds of bacteria and test engineered genomes with new abilities. This could speed up designing bacteria that help in many areas, such as making new drugs or cleaning the environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have revived dead bacterial cells by replacing their DNA, opening new ways to engineer bacteria for useful purposes.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques réaniment des bactéries mortes en échangeant leur ADN
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques travaillent à modifier des bactéries en remplaçant leur ADN par celui d'autres bactéries. Cela permet de créer des bactéries capables de tâches utiles, comme fabriquer des médicaments ou du carburant. Mais cela a été difficile avec beaucoup de types de bactéries.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs de l’Institut J. Craig Venter a réussi à redonner vie à des cellules bactériennes mortes en insérant un génome complet d’une autre espèce. Ils ont utilisé un produit chimique pour arrêter les bactéries d’origine, puis ont introduit un ADN nouveau d’une bactérie proche. Certaines de ces cellules « mortes » ont recommencé à fonctionner et à se développer normalement.

**Impact** – C’est important car cette méthode est plus sûre et plus précise. Elle évite les faux résultats où les bactéries mélangeaient l’ADN de façon inattendue. Maintenant, les scientifiques peuvent essayer avec d’autres bactéries, y compris des bactéries communes en laboratoire comme E. coli, ce qui ouvre plus de possibilités pour créer des bactéries utiles en médecine ou énergie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont tester cette méthode avec d’autres bactéries et essayer des génomes modifiés avec de nouvelles fonctions. Cela pourrait accélérer la création de bactéries aidant dans plusieurs domaines, comme fabriquer des médicaments ou nettoyer l’environnement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont réanimé des bactéries mortes en remplaçant leur ADN, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour modifier les bactéries à des fins utiles.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos reviven bacterias muertas al cambiar su ADN
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estado trabajando para cambiar bacterias reemplazando su ADN con material genético de otras bacterias. Esto ayuda a crear bacterias que pueden hacer trabajos útiles, como producir medicinas o combustibles. Pero ha sido difícil hacerlo con muchos tipos de bacterias.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores del Instituto J. Craig Venter encontró una forma de devolver la vida a células bacterianas muertas al poner un genoma completo de otra especie. Usaron un químico para detener las bacterias originales, luego insertaron ADN nuevo de una bacteria relacionada. Algunas de estas células “muertas” volvieron a funcionar y a crecer como bacterias normales.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra un método nuevo para cambiar bacterias de forma más segura y precisa. Evita resultados falsos que pasaban antes cuando las bacterias mezclaban ADN de manera inesperada. Ahora los científicos pueden probar esto con diferentes bacterias, incluso con bacterias comunes de laboratorio como E. coli, lo que da más oportunidades para crear bacterias útiles para medicina o energía.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probarán este método de transplante de genomas con otros tipos de bacterias y evaluarán genomas diseñados con nuevas habilidades. Esto podría acelerar el diseño de bacterias que ayuden en muchas áreas, como fabricar nuevos medicamentos o limpiar el medio ambiente.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos han revivido células bacterianas muertas reemplazando su ADN, abriendo nuevas formas de diseñar bacterias para usos útiles.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, translucent bacterial cell silhouette being gently infused with a vibrant, intricately patterned strand of DNA resembling a delicate, twisting ribbon, set against a soft, muted background that suggests revival and transformation through subtle light and shadow.

articles.title: Antimatter has been transported for the first time ever — in the back of CERN's truck
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00950-wPhysicists have succeeded for the first time in transporting the most expensive and most volatile substance on Earth — antimatter.
articles.content:
Scientists successfully transported 92 antiprotons in a truck around CERN, Europe’s particle physics laboratory, using specialized bottles which prevent contact with matter.Credit: CERN

Antimatter is matter’s equal and opposite. If the two meet they annihilate each other, turning entirely into energy, which makes it incredible difficult to store or move antimatter.

On 24 March, a team at CERN, the European particle physics laboratory, transported 92 antiprotons in a specially designed bottle that traps the particles using magnetic fields. The bottle travelled on the back of a truck, for a 30-minute journey around the lab’s site outside Geneva, Switzerland.

The experiment’s ultimate goal is to take the antiparticles to a location free from experimental noise, where antiprotons can be studied in greater precision than is possible in the CERN “antimatter factory” where they are created.

CERN is the only place in the world that produces antiprotons in usable quantities. Many staff turned out with their phone cameras to capture the truck as it travelled more than 8 kilometres around the site, reaching a maximum speed of 42 km per hour.

“It is something humanity has never done before, it is historic,” says Stefan Ulmer, a physicist at Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU), in Germany, and member of the team. “We bought a lot of champagne, and we invited the entire antimatter community to celebrate with us today.”

Antimatter can be used to study other phenomena such as the structure of radioactive nuclei, or researched itself to unravel some of the Universe’s deepest mysteries. Physicists who created the antimatter factory more than thirty years ago dreamed that someday it might be possible to transport it, says Christian Smorra, a physicist at HHU who led the project. “Now it’s finally possible.”

“This is a great technological achievement,” says Tara Shears, a physicist at the University of Liverpool, UK. Antimatter is the most fragile type of matter there is, and so storing it, let alone driving it around CERN, is “a technological marvel”, she says.

“I love the idea of CERN becoming the Deliveroo of antimatter,” she adds.

Antimatter deliveroo

Anti-particles are like their matter counterparts, except with their charge and magnetic properties reversed. Although matter is abundant, antimatter occurs naturally only very rarely. No one knows why this disparity exists, when both should have been created in equal amounts during the Big Bang.

CERN makes antimatter by colliding beams of protons into a dense metal, then capturing and slowing the antiprotons that emerge using electric and magnetic fields. The process is painstaking, and most particles are lost in the process.

Simulation of matter–antimatter creation on quantum platforms

Developing a portable trap for the particles where the particles never touch the matter-containing sides – meant powering a superconducting magnet system and using cryogenics to cool it to a chilly 4 kelvin (−269 °C). The bottle had to be kept in a very strict vacuum to stop antimatter meeting any stray matter particles and annihilating on the way, and all the kit had to withstand the forces of the journey in a truck. The team installed a detector, which meant they could check on the antiprotons from the driving seat.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a historic scientific and technological achievement: the first successful transportation of antimatter, a highly volatile and valuable substance. This breakthrough has broad implications for fundamental physics research, potentially enabling more precise studies of antimatter and the universe's origins. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a significant advance with meaningful impact for science and society.
articles.personality_title: Scientists transport antimatter for the first time at CERN
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Antimatter is a rare and very fragile substance that is the opposite of normal matter. When antimatter touches matter, both disappear in a burst of energy. Because of this, antimatter is hard to store or move safely. CERN, a famous physics lab in Europe, makes antimatter but has never moved it before.

**What happened** – On March 24, 2026, scientists at CERN successfully transported 92 antiprotons, tiny particles of antimatter, inside a special bottle. This bottle uses magnetic fields to keep antimatter from touching anything else. The bottle was carried by truck for 30 minutes on an 8-kilometer trip around CERN’s site near Geneva, Switzerland.

**Impact** – This is the first time anyone has moved antimatter safely. Moving antimatter means scientists can now study it in quieter places away from the busy lab, which can help them understand more about the universe. The technology needed to keep antimatter stable during movement is very advanced, involving super cold temperatures and strong magnets to keep it trapped.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to use this ability to transport antimatter to conduct more detailed experiments. This could help answer big questions, like why there is more matter than antimatter in the universe. The success also shows new ways to handle antimatter for future research.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time ever, scientists safely moved antimatter around CERN, opening new possibilities for studying the universe’s mysteries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques transportent pour la première fois de l’antimatière au CERN
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’antimatière est une substance rare et très fragile, opposée à la matière normale. Quand l’antimatière touche la matière, elles disparaissent toutes les deux en libérant de l’énergie. Cela rend l’antimatière difficile à stocker ou à déplacer en toute sécurité. Le CERN, un grand laboratoire de physique en Europe, fabrique de l’antimatière mais ne l’avait jamais déplacée auparavant.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 24 mars 2026, des scientifiques du CERN ont réussi à transporter 92 antiprotons, des particules d’antimatière, dans une bouteille spéciale. Cette bouteille utilise des champs magnétiques pour empêcher l’antimatière de toucher quoi que ce soit. La bouteille a été transportée en camion pendant 30 minutes sur un trajet de 8 kilomètres autour du site du CERN près de Genève, en Suisse.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que l’on déplace de l’antimatière en toute sécurité. Cela permet aux chercheurs de l’étudier dans des endroits plus calmes, loin du laboratoire bruyant, ce qui aidera à mieux comprendre l’univers. La technologie utilisée est très avancée, avec des températures très froides et des aimants puissants pour garder l’antimatière piégée.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent utiliser ce transport pour faire des expériences plus précises. Cela pourrait aider à répondre à de grandes questions, comme pourquoi il y a plus de matière que d’antimatière dans l’univers. Ce succès montre aussi de nouvelles façons de manipuler l’antimatière pour la recherche future.

**Une phrase clé** – Pour la première fois, des scientifiques ont déplacé en toute sécurité de l’antimatière au CERN, ouvrant la voie à de nouvelles découvertes sur les mystères de l’univers.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos transportan antimateria por primera vez en el CERN
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La antimateria es una sustancia rara y muy frágil, opuesta a la materia normal. Cuando la antimateria toca la materia, ambas desaparecen en una explosión de energía. Por eso es difícil almacenar o mover antimateria de forma segura. El CERN, un famoso laboratorio de física en Europa, produce antimateria pero nunca la había transportado.

**Qué pasó** – El 24 de marzo de 2026, científicos del CERN lograron transportar 92 antiprotones, pequeñas partículas de antimateria, dentro de una botella especial. Esta botella usa campos magnéticos para evitar que la antimateria toque cualquier otra cosa. La botella viajó en un camión durante 30 minutos en un recorrido de 8 kilómetros alrededor del sitio del CERN cerca de Ginebra, Suiza.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que alguien mueve antimateria de forma segura. Esto permite a los científicos estudiarla en lugares más tranquilos, lejos del laboratorio ruidoso, lo que puede ayudar a entender mejor el universo. La tecnología para mantener la antimateria estable durante el transporte es muy avanzada, usando temperaturas muy frías y potentes imanes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar esta capacidad para hacer experimentos más detallados. Esto podría ayudar a responder grandes preguntas, como por qué hay más materia que antimateria en el universo. El éxito también muestra nuevas formas de manejar antimateria para futuras investigaciones.

**Una frase clave** – Por primera vez, los científicos movieron antimateria de forma segura en el CERN, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para estudiar los misterios del universo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a sleek, futuristic bottle glowing softly with swirling magnetic field lines inside, securely nestled on the back of a stylized truck gently moving along a winding path around a serene laboratory landscape, all rendered in warm, natural tones with subtle hints of cool blues to evoke cryogenic cold, capturing the delicate and historic journey of antimatter safely transported without any human figures.
